result:
The concept of "deep time" is something that constantly hums in the back of my mind. We live our lives in such frantic, compressed intervals—seconds, minutes, deadlines, and digital notifications—that we often lose sight of the geological scale beneath our feet. If you look at a mountain range or even a jagged piece of shoreline, you are witnessing a slow-motion collision of forces that have been grinding away for millions of years. 

There is a strange, paradoxical comfort in realizing how small our immediate anxieties are when measured against the lifespan of a tectonic plate or the cooling of a star. It doesn’t make our emotions less real; rather, it makes them more precious. We are flickering sparks of consciousness in a universe that spent eons arranging the conditions necessary for us to exist, to wonder, and to reflect on our own existence. 

This perspective shift can change how you walk through the world. A cup of coffee in the morning isn't just caffeine; it’s an intersection of global trade, volcanic soil, and the simple, quiet miracle of having a moment to pause before the day accelerates again. Maybe the secret to a meaningful life isn’t about leaving a monumental legacy that lasts forever, but about being fully present in the infinitesimal slice of time we are granted. We are here to witness the light, to solve the puzzles we find, and to leave behind a little more kindness than we started with, before returning our atoms to the silent, swirling expanse of the cosmos.
